message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

content.text: <ide_opened_file>The user opened the file /Users/albert/Projects/philo/instructions.md in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>
help me brainstorm how to measure hackability of reward rubrics (axis 2)

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Let me read the project context before brainstorming so my ideas fit what you're actually building.

content.content:
1	CONFIDENTIAL - DO NOT DISTRIBUTE
2	PHILO LABS
3	Research Engineer / Scientist Take-Home
4	Track R: Evaluating Rubrics for Multimodal Agentic RL
5	~4 hours | 2 calendar days | Jun 2026
6	Context
7	Math has formal proof. Code has unit tests. They are trustworthy because people built them on
8	purpose.
9	For knowledge work, creative work, and most tasks that matter, we have no such thing. We write
10	rubrics instead: short lists of criteria, each scored by a person or by a model acting as a judge.
11	The problem: a rubric can look like it grades quality while it really grades form. A memo can pass
12	every line and still say nothing. A strong analysis can fail lines that have nothing to do with whether it
13	is strong.
14	On a leaderboard, that is a small problem. As an RL reward, it compounds. The policy learns to
15	satisfy the checklist without doing the work, and the rubric quietly teaches the model the wrong thing.
16	So before we trust a rubric as training signal, we have to grade the rubric itself. This take-home asks
17	you to build that grader.
18	Why GDPval
19	You will work from GDPval (Patwardhan et al., 2025), OpenAI's open benchmark of real,
20	economically valuable knowledge work. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gdpval
21	•
22	•
23	•
24	•
25	Scale. 1,320 tasks across 44 occupations and 9 sectors. A gold subset of 220 is public.
26	The right shape. Tasks are multimodal. Many are exactly our kind: video understanding
27	(detectives, private investigators), video and image editing (film directors, VFX artists), plus
28	documents, slides, and spreadsheets.
29	What ships with each task. A prompt, reference files, a gold deliverable made by an
30	experienced professional, and a grading rubric.
31	Why that helps. The gold deliverable is a free oracle. It is exactly what you need to test
32	whether a rubric is calibrated.
33	The Task
34	Build two things and run them end to end on a slice of GDPval:
35	•
36	a rubric evaluator that scores how good a rubric is along the axes below, and
37	•
38	an automatic iterator that rewrites a rubric to raise those scores.
39	Use your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, or similar) heavily throughout.
40	Part 1: Get familiar with 10 tasks
41	We have chosen the 10 tasks for you. They are all video-centric on purpose. Video is where rubrics
42	are weakest, where we work, and the part candidates tend to avoid. Use these exact tasks. Do not
43	swap in document or slide tasks.
44	Occupation GDPval task folder
45	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__75401f7c
46	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8
47	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__c94452e4
48	Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__e222075d
49	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__38889c3b
50	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__4b894ae3
51	Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__ff85ee58
52	Private detectives and
53	investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2
54	Private detectives and
55	investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2
56	Producers and directors producers_and_directors__e4f664ea
57	The slice spans both capability families we care about:
58	•
59	•
60	Editing. Film and video editors, audio and video technicians, and producers and directors. The
61	output is an edited or produced video.
62	Video understanding. Private detectives and investigators. The work is to review footage and
63	find what matters.
64	Part 2: The rubric scorecard
65	This is the heart of the take-home, and the part we will read most closely. For each rubric, score it
66	along the axes below.
67	Treat them as a standing test suite. A rubric that fails any one is suspect, no matter how reasonable
68	it looks on the page. The list is not complete on purpose: finding the axes we have not named is part
69	of the work.
70	Axis 1: Boundary calibration
71	The cheap checks, run first. Two sanity anchors:
72	•
73	Gold scores near 1. Run the rubric on the gold deliverable.
74	•
75	Null scores near 0. Run it on a do-nothing output: an empty file, the unedited input, or a black
76	frame.
77	Axis 2: Reward hackability
78	This is the axis that matters most for RL. A rubric is hackable if a policy can raise its score without
79	improving the work. The policy will optimize for exactly what you reward, including the parts you did
80	not mean.
81	The classic trap is a criterion that only checks that something is present. Take a captioning rubric
82	with the line "the caption is visible."
83	A policy can satisfy it with a huge caption that covers the speaker's face. Visible, yes, but worse, not
84	better. The line states a presence condition with no matching absence condition.
85	The fix is to pair them: "the caption is visible AND does not cover the main subject." Presence plus
86	absence closes the gap.
87	That is one pattern. There are more, and we left them for you to find.
88	Axis 3: Consistency
89	A rubric is only useful if it returns the same verdict on the same work.
90	•
91	Self-consistency: run the same rubric on the same output several times with the same judge.
92	Do the scores reproduce, or wander?
93	•
94	Cross-model consistency: score the same output with two or more judge models. Do they
95	agree?
96	When two judges disagree, ask why. If one judge is simply more capable, fine. If a criterion is so
97	vague that two models read it differently, that is a rubric defect: fix the wording, do not blame the
98	judge.
99	And more (open on purpose)
100	We expect you to add axes we have not named. Strong candidates:
101	•
102	Discrimination: does it separate excellent work from merely adequate, not just good from
103	broken?
104	•
105	Coverage: does it grade what the task is actually about, or only the easy-to-name surface?
106	•
107	Saturation: how does it behave once every strong model passes it?
108	Part 3: The iterator
109	Build a loop that improves a rubric automatically:
110	•
111	evaluate the rubric on the axes above,
112	•
113	diagnose which axis it fails, and why,
114	•
115	rewrite the rubric to fix that failure,
116	•
117	evaluate again.
118	Run it on your 10 tasks. For each, report before and after: did hackability drop, did cross-model
119	agreement rise where it should, did the gold move toward 1 and the null toward 0?
120	Be frank about what moved and what did not. A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.
121	Part 4: Report
122	1,500-2,500 words in LaTeX, compiled to PDF with your coding agent. Cover:
123	•
124	the framing of your scorecard and your design choices,
125	•
126	what you learned running it on GDPval,
127	•
128	most importantly, an honest account of which rubrics you would trust as RL reward and which
129	you would not, and which axes you believe generalize to rubrics you never saw.
130	Deliverables
131	Deliverable Details
132	Report (PDF) LaTeX compiled to PDF via your coding agent. 1,500-2,500 words.
133	Code GitHub repo (preferred) or zip. Clean README with run instructions
134	and the agent prompts you used.
135	rubric_scorecard.tsv
136	Auto-generated by the pipeline. One row per (task x rubric version),
137	with a column for each axis (gold score, null score, hackability labels,
138	self-consistency variance, cross-model agreement) and a column
139	flagging suspected reward hacking. Reproducible with a single
140	command.
141	10 rubric packages For each task: the original rubric, the rewritten rubric, the test cases
142	used, and the hackability analysis. Released as one folder per task.
143	Agent session
144	Exported coding agent session. For Claude Code, run /export, or use
145	claude-code-transcripts to export as HTML. For other agents, export
146	the full session log per your tool's docs.
147	Evaluation
148	•
149	Systematic thinking (40%): a coherent framework, first-principles reasoning about what
150	makes a rubric trustworthy as training signal, and clear thinking about reward hacking and
151	about what a rubric fails to cover.
152	•
153	•
154	•
155	Rubric-evaluation quality (25%): axes that are precise, measurable, implementable from your
156	spec alone, and honest about which ones would survive being used as reward.
157	Implementation (20%): clean, modular, end to end. The evaluator and iterator run with a single
158	command, and the rubric packages are self-contained.
159	Agent fluency (15%): effective use of coding agents across the full workflow.
160	Logistics
161	•
162	•
163	•
164	•
165	Time: ~4 focused hours, 2 calendar days from receipt.
166	Resources: anything publicly available. The GDPval gold subset is on Hugging Face. API and
167	compute costs reimbursed up to $200 (note your spend).
168	Questions: email us. Good questions are a positive signal.
169	Submit: email a GitHub link or a zip containing all deliverables.
170	References
171	•
172	•
173	•
174	GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks.
175	Patwardhan et al., 2025 (OpenAI). The benchmark and gold task subset you will work from.
176	arXiv:2510.04374
177	Rubrics as Rewards: Reinforcement Learning Beyond Verifiable Domains. Gunjal et al.,
178	2025. Uses rubric feedback as an on-policy RL reward and studies how to turn rubric scores
179	into a reward. Precedent for treating rubrics as the reward signal. arXiv:2507.17746
180	AdvancedIF and RIFL: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and RL for Instruction Following. He
181	et al., 2025 (Meta Superintelligence Labs). An expert-curated rubric benchmark plus a pipeline
182	of rubric generation, a rubric verifier, and reward shaping. Precedent for the generator, verifier,
183	and updater shape. arXiv:2511.10507
184	This document is confidential. Do not share or post publicly.
185

CONFIDENTIAL - DO NOT DISTRIBUTE
PHILO LABS
Research Engineer / Scientist Take-Home
Track R: Evaluating Rubrics for Multimodal Agentic RL
~4 hours | 2 calendar days | Jun 2026
Context
Math has formal proof. Code has unit tests. They are trustworthy because people built them on
purpose.
For knowledge work, creative work, and most tasks that matter, we have no such thing. We write
rubrics instead: short lists of criteria, each scored by a person or by a model acting as a judge.
The problem: a rubric can look like it grades quality while it really grades form. A memo can pass
every line and still say nothing. A strong analysis can fail lines that have nothing to do with whether it
is strong.
On a leaderboard, that is a small problem. As an RL reward, it compounds. The policy learns to
satisfy the checklist without doing the work, and the rubric quietly teaches the model the wrong thing.
So before we trust a rubric as training signal, we have to grade the rubric itself. This take-home asks
you to build that grader.
Why GDPval
You will work from GDPval (Patwardhan et al., 2025), OpenAI's open benchmark of real,
economically valuable knowledge work. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gdpval
•
•
•
•
Scale. 1,320 tasks across 44 occupations and 9 sectors. A gold subset of 220 is public.
The right shape. Tasks are multimodal. Many are exactly our kind: video understanding
(detectives, private investigators), video and image editing (film directors, VFX artists), plus
documents, slides, and spreadsheets.
What ships with each task. A prompt, reference files, a gold deliverable made by an
experienced professional, and a grading rubric.
Why that helps. The gold deliverable is a free oracle. It is exactly what you need to test
whether a rubric is calibrated.
The Task
Build two things and run them end to end on a slice of GDPval:
•
a rubric evaluator that scores how good a rubric is along the axes below, and
•
an automatic iterator that rewrites a rubric to raise those scores.
Use your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, or similar) heavily throughout.
Part 1: Get familiar with 10 tasks
We have chosen the 10 tasks for you. They are all video-centric on purpose. Video is where rubrics
are weakest, where we work, and the part candidates tend to avoid. Use these exact tasks. Do not
swap in document or slide tasks.
Occupation GDPval task folder
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__75401f7c
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__c94452e4
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__e222075d

Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__4b894ae3
Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__ff85ee58
Private detectives and
investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2
Private detectives and
investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2
Producers and directors producers_and_directors__e4f664ea
The slice spans both capability families we care about:
•
•
Editing. Film and video editors, audio and video technicians, and producers and directors. The
output is an edited or produced video.
Video understanding. Private detectives and investigators. The work is to review footage and
find what matters.
Part 2: The rubric scorecard
This is the heart of the take-home, and the part we will read most closely. For each rubric, score it
along the axes below.
Treat them as a standing test suite. A rubric that fails any one is suspect, no matter how reasonable
it looks on the page. The list is not complete on purpose: finding the axes we have not named is part
of the work.
Axis 1: Boundary calibration
The cheap checks, run first. Two sanity anchors:
•
Gold scores near 1. Run the rubric on the gold deliverable.
•
Null scores near 0. Run it on a do-nothing output: an empty file, the unedited input, or a black
frame.
Axis 2: Reward hackability
This is the axis that matters most for RL. A rubric is hackable if a policy can raise its score without
improving the work. The policy will optimize for exactly what you reward, including the parts you did
not mean.
The classic trap is a criterion that only checks that something is present. Take a captioning rubric
with the line "the caption is visible."
A policy can satisfy it with a huge caption that covers the speaker's face. Visible, yes, but worse, not
better. The line states a presence condition with no matching absence condition.
The fix is to pair them: "the caption is visible AND does not cover the main subject." Presence plus
absence closes the gap.
That is one pattern. There are more, and we left them for you to find.
Axis 3: Consistency
A rubric is only useful if it returns the same verdict on the same work.
•
Self-consistency: run the same rubric on the same output several times with the same judge.
Do the scores reproduce, or wander?
•
Cross-model consistency: score the same output with two or more judge models. Do they
agree?
When two judges disagree, ask why. If one judge is simply more capable, fine. If a criterion is so
vague that two models read it differently, that is a rubric defect: fix the wording, do not blame the
judge.
And more (open on purpose)
We expect you to add axes we have not named. Strong candidates:
•
Discrimination: does it separate excellent work from merely adequate, not just good from
broken?
•
Coverage: does it grade what the task is actually about, or only the easy-to-name surface?
•
Saturation: how does it behave once every strong model passes it?
Part 3: The iterator
Build a loop that improves a rubric automatically:
•
evaluate the rubric on the axes above,
•
diagnose which axis it fails, and why,
•
rewrite the rubric to fix that failure,
•
evaluate again.
Run it on your 10 tasks. For each, report before and after: did hackability drop, did cross-model
agreement rise where it should, did the gold move toward 1 and the null toward 0?
Be frank about what moved and what did not. A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.
Part 4: Report
1,500-2,500 words in LaTeX, compiled to PDF with your coding agent. Cover:
•
the framing of your scorecard and your design choices,
•
what you learned running it on GDPval,
•
most importantly, an honest account of which rubrics you would trust as RL reward and which
you would not, and which axes you believe generalize to rubrics you never saw.
Deliverables
Deliverable Details
Report (PDF) LaTeX compiled to PDF via your coding agent. 1,500-2,500 words.
Code GitHub repo (preferred) or zip. Clean README with run instructions
and the agent prompts you used.
rubric_scorecard.tsv
Auto-generated by the pipeline. One row per (task x rubric version),
with a column for each axis (gold score, null score, hackability labels,
self-consistency variance, cross-model agreement) and a column
flagging suspected reward hacking. Reproducible with a single
command.

used, and the hackability analysis. Released as one folder per task.
Agent session

the full session log per your tool's docs.
Evaluation
•

makes a rubric trustworthy as training signal, and clear thinking about reward hacking and
about what a rubric fails to cover.

•

spec alone, and honest about which ones would survive being used as reward.
Implementation (20%): clean, modular, end to end. The evaluator and iterator run with a single
command, and the rubric packages are self-contained.

content.content:
1	# Progress Report — Rubric Scorecard for GDPval
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Building a rubric evaluator and iterator for 10 video-centric GDPval tasks (Philo Labs take-home). The evaluator scores rubrics along Axis 1 (boundary calibration), Axis 3 (consistency), and derived metrics (leaks, gaps, unverifiable items). A browser viewer and automated rollout pipeline support the full workflow.
6	
7	## What Exists
8	
9	### Data Layer
10	- **10 tasks** downloaded into `data/` from the GDPval gold subset, covering 4 occupations across 2 capability families (editing: film/video/audio editors + producers; understanding: private detectives).
11	- 6 of 10 have released gold deliverables; 4 are gold-free (no ground-truth output to score against yet).
12	- Manifest at `data/manifest.json` drives the viewer.
13	
14	### Viewer (`viewer/`)
15	- Static HTML/CSS/JS single-page app: sidebar task list → tabs (Prompt, Rubric, Reference Files, Gold Deliverable, Rollouts).
16	- Inline video/audio/image/PDF/text preview; rubric parsed from JSON into sortable table with score pills and tags.
17	- Fetches rollouts manifest from `eval/rollouts_manifest.json` (optional — graceful degradation when absent).
18	- Dark theme, sticky headers, responsive layout.
19	
20	### Eval Framework (`eval/`)
21	
22	**Philosophy**: Give the judge a fair shot. Early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to things the judge simply couldn't see (codec, resolution, runtime, exact wording). Deterministic fact tools (`probe_media`, `file_facts`) and reference-file access close that gap — the remaining 3% gap is genuine rubric defects.
23	
24	**Judge Suite** (cheap, varied by 3 axes):
25	| key | model | modalities | role |
26	|-----|-------|-----------|------|
27	| `gpt-mini` | gpt-5.4-mini | text+image | strong vision anchor |
28	| `gpt-nano` | gpt-5.4-nano | text+image | capability ablation vs mini |
29	| `qwen-vl` | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text+image | cross-vendor vision |
30	| `qwen-text` | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text-only | modality ablation (no vision) |
31	| `gemini-lite` | gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text+image+audio | only native audio judge |
32	| `nemotron-omni` | nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text+image+audio | free 2nd native audio |
33	
34	**Modes**: `--mode judge` (score rubrics on subjects: gold, null blank, null unedited_input, or rollout deliverables) and `--mode rollout` (policy agents attempt real tasks, editing media via sandboxed ffmpeg).
35	
36	**Null inputs**: Auto-generated per task — blank (5s black+silence / empty PDF) and unedited_input (source as-is). Two cheap anchors to calibrate the bottom of the scale.
37	
38	**Scoring**: Per-item verdicts (met/unmet/partial/unverifiable) → normalized score (positive ceiling) and `verifiable_only` score (dropping uncheckable items). Penalty items floor at 0.
39	
40	### Results (`eval/results/`)
41	
42	**Axis 1 (Boundary Calibration)** — 6 tasks × 3 judges (gpt-mini, qwen-vl, gemini-lite):
43	
44	| Task | Gold (avg) | Worst Null (avg) | Gap (avg) | Leaks (avg) | Neg-gap judges |
45	|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
46	| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.622 | 0.008 | **0.614** | 0.7 | 1/3 |
47	| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.484 | 0.036 | **0.448** | 1.3 | 1/3 |
48	| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.630 | 0.147 | **0.483** | 5.3 | 0/3 |
49	| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.366 | 0.054 | **0.312** | 1.7 | 1/3 |
50	| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 0.630 | 0.412 | **0.218** | 20.7 | 1/3 |
51	| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.000 | 0.055 | **-0.055** | 1.7 | **2/3** |
52	
53	Key findings:
54	- **`e222075d`** (green energy ad) scores zero on gold across all judges — gold deliverable may be missing or the rubric fundamentally broken.
55	- **`57b2cdf2`** has 20.7 avg leaks — the null `unedited_input` scores 0.644 with gpt-mini, meaning half the rubric items reward presence of form, not quality.
56	- **gemini-lite** shows a systematic pattern: scores gold near 0 on several tasks but gives reasonable null scores → gap often negative or near-zero (4/6 tasks). Likely a calibration/capability issue rather than rubric defect.
57	- **qwen-vl** has the tightest null scores (avg 0.021) and widest gaps (avg 0.514) — best boundary discriminator of the three.
58	
59	**Axis 3 (Consistency)** — only 1 task scored so far:
60	
61	| Type | Task | Panel | Mean | Stdev | Frac Unanimous | Item Stdev |
62	|---|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
63	| Cross-model | `57b2cdf2` | gpt-nano+qwen-text | 0.836 | 0.147 | 0.689 | 0.173 |
64	| Self-consistency | `57b2cdf2` | gpt-nano, 3× | 0.718 | 0.023 | 0.889 | 0.058 |
65	
66	Self-consistency is high (stdev 0.023, 89% unanimous). Cross-model shows a 0.295 gap between gpt-nano (0.688) and qwen-text (0.983) — driven by 8 criteria where one model passes and the other doesn't, all about "mentions photographic evidence of [event]" style items (vague wording → contested).
67	
68	## What's Next
69	
70	1. **Full Axis 3 sweep**: Run cross-model and self-consistency on all 6 gold tasks.
71	2. **Rollouts**: Generate policy deliverables for all 10 tasks (especially the 4 without gold) to enable Axis 1/3 on real model outputs.
72	3. **Rubric iterator (Part 3)**: Build the auto-rewrite loop that diagnoses failures and repairs rubrics, tracking before/after scores.
73	4. **Reward hackability analysis (Axis 2)**: Systematic audit of presence-vs-absence patterns and other hackable structures.
74	5. **Additional axes**: Discrimination (separates excellent from adequate), coverage (grades actual task content vs surface), saturation (behavior at ceiling).
75	6. **Report (Part 4)**: 1500-2500 word LaTeX PDF.
76	7. **Package deliverables**: rubric_scorecard.tsv, 10 rubric packages per task.
77

file.content:
# Progress Report — Rubric Scorecard for GDPval

## Overview

Building a rubric evaluator and iterator for 10 video-centric GDPval tasks (Philo Labs take-home). The evaluator scores rubrics along Axis 1 (boundary calibration), Axis 3 (consistency), and derived metrics (leaks, gaps, unverifiable items). A browser viewer and automated rollout pipeline support the full workflow.

## What Exists

### Data Layer
- **10 tasks** downloaded into `data/` from the GDPval gold subset, covering 4 occupations across 2 capability families (editing: film/video/audio editors + producers; understanding: private detectives).
- 6 of 10 have released gold deliverables; 4 are gold-free (no ground-truth output to score against yet).
- Manifest at `data/manifest.json` drives the viewer.

### Viewer (`viewer/`)
- Static HTML/CSS/JS single-page app: sidebar task list → tabs (Prompt, Rubric, Reference Files, Gold Deliverable, Rollouts).
- Inline video/audio/image/PDF/text preview; rubric parsed from JSON into sortable table with score pills and tags.
- Fetches rollouts manifest from `eval/rollouts_manifest.json` (optional — graceful degradation when absent).
- Dark theme, sticky headers, responsive layout.

### Eval Framework (`eval/`)

**Philosophy**: Give the judge a fair shot. Early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to things the judge simply couldn't see (codec, resolution, runtime, exact wording). Deterministic fact tools (`probe_media`, `file_facts`) and reference-file access close that gap — the remaining 3% gap is genuine rubric defects.

**Judge Suite** (cheap, varied by 3 axes):
| key | model | modalities | role |
|-----|-------|-----------|------|
| `gpt-mini` | gpt-5.4-mini | text+image | strong vision anchor |
| `gpt-nano` | gpt-5.4-nano | text+image | capability ablation vs mini |
| `qwen-vl` | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text+image | cross-vendor vision |
| `qwen-text` | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text-only | modality ablation (no vision) |
| `gemini-lite` | gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text+image+audio | only native audio judge |
| `nemotron-omni` | nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text+image+audio | free 2nd native audio |

**Modes**: `--mode judge` (score rubrics on subjects: gold, null blank, null unedited_input, or rollout deliverables) and `--mode rollout` (policy agents attempt real tasks, editing media via sandboxed ffmpeg).

**Null inputs**: Auto-generated per task — blank (5s black+silence / empty PDF) and unedited_input (source as-is). Two cheap anchors to calibrate the bottom of the scale.

**Scoring**: Per-item verdicts (met/unmet/partial/unverifiable) → normalized score (positive ceiling) and `verifiable_only` score (dropping uncheckable items). Penalty items floor at 0.

### Results (`eval/results/`)

**Axis 1 (Boundary Calibration)** — 6 tasks × 3 judges (gpt-mini, qwen-vl, gemini-lite):

| Task | Gold (avg) | Worst Null (avg) | Gap (avg) | Leaks (avg) | Neg-gap judges |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.622 | 0.008 | **0.614** | 0.7 | 1/3 |
| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.484 | 0.036 | **0.448** | 1.3 | 1/3 |
| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.630 | 0.147 | **0.483** | 5.3 | 0/3 |
| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.366 | 0.054 | **0.312** | 1.7 | 1/3 |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 0.630 | 0.412 | **0.218** | 20.7 | 1/3 |
| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.000 | 0.055 | **-0.055** | 1.7 | **2/3** |

Key findings:
- **`e222075d`** (green energy ad) scores zero on gold across all judges — gold deliverable may be missing or the rubric fundamentally broken.
- **`57b2cdf2`** has 20.7 avg leaks — the null `unedited_input` scores 0.644 with gpt-mini, meaning half the rubric items reward presence of form, not quality.
- **gemini-lite** shows a systematic pattern: scores gold near 0 on several tasks but gives reasonable null scores → gap often negative or near-zero (4/6 tasks). Likely a calibration/capability issue rather than rubric defect.
- **qwen-vl** has the tightest null scores (avg 0.021) and widest gaps (avg 0.514) — best boundary discriminator of the three.

**Axis 3 (Consistency)** — only 1 task scored so far:

| Type | Task | Panel | Mean | Stdev | Frac Unanimous | Item Stdev |
|---|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Cross-model | `57b2cdf2` | gpt-nano+qwen-text | 0.836 | 0.147 | 0.689 | 0.173 |
| Self-consistency | `57b2cdf2` | gpt-nano, 3× | 0.718 | 0.023 | 0.889 | 0.058 |

Self-consistency is high (stdev 0.023, 89% unanimous). Cross-model shows a 0.295 gap between gpt-nano (0.688) and qwen-text (0.983) — driven by 8 criteria where one model passes and the other doesn't, all about "mentions photographic evidence of [event]" style items (vague wording → contested).

## What's Next

1. **Full Axis 3 sweep**: Run cross-model and self-consistency on all 6 gold tasks.
2. **Rollouts**: Generate policy deliverables for all 10 tasks (especially the 4 without gold) to enable Axis 1/3 on real model outputs.
3. **Rubric iterator (Part 3)**: Build the auto-rewrite loop that diagnoses failures and repairs rubrics, tracking before/after scores.
4. **Reward hackability analysis (Axis 2)**: Systematic audit of presence-vs-absence patterns and other hackable structures.
5. **Additional axes**: Discrimination (separates excellent from adequate), coverage (grades actual task content vs surface), saturation (behavior at ceiling).
6. **Report (Part 4)**: 1500-2500 word LaTeX PDF.
7. **Package deliverables**: rubric_scorecard.tsv, 10 rubric packages per task.

1	"""Rubric loading and scorecard aggregation.
2	
3	A rubric is a list of items, each worth `score` points for a binary/quality
4	criterion. The judge awards each item a fraction in [0,1] of its points; the
5	normalized rubric score is (sum of awarded points) / (sum of max points).
6	"""
7	from __future__ import annotations
8	
9	import json
10	from dataclasses import dataclass
11	from pathlib import Path
12	
13	from . import config
14	
15	
16	@dataclass
17	class RubricItem:
18	    id: str
19	    criterion: str
20	    max_score: float
21	
22	
23	def load_rubric(task_folder: str) -> list[RubricItem]:
24	    raw = json.loads((config.DATA / task_folder / "rubric.json").read_text())
25	    items = []
26	    for r in raw:
27	        items.append(RubricItem(
28	            id=r["rubric_item_id"],
29	            criterion=r["criterion"].strip(),
30	            max_score=float(r["score"]),
31	        ))
32	    return items
33	
34	
35	def positive_max(items: list[RubricItem]) -> float:
36	    """Best achievable score: all positive items earned, no penalties triggered."""
37	    return sum(i.max_score for i in items if i.max_score > 0)
38	
39	
40	def normalize(items: list[RubricItem], awarded: dict[str, float],
41	              unverifiable: set[str] | None = None) -> dict:
42	    """awarded: item_id -> fraction in [0,1] of the *extent the criterion is true*.
43	
44	    For positive items, fraction is credit earned. For penalty items (negative
45	    points), fraction is how strongly the (bad) condition holds, so a triggered
46	    penalty subtracts points. The deliverable's score is divided by the positive
47	    ceiling and clamped to [0,1]; a deliverable that trips heavy penalties floors
48	    at 0 rather than going negative.
49	
50	    `unverifiable` items (the judge couldn't check them at all) still count as 0
51	    in `normalized`, but are removed from BOTH numerator and denominator in
52	    `normalized_verifiable_only` — the fair score given what was actually checkable.
53	    A large gap between the two means the rubric asks for things no judge can
54	    verify from the deliverable (a coverage/verifiability defect).
55	    """
56	    unverifiable = unverifiable or set()
57	    ceiling = positive_max(items)
58	    ceiling_verif = sum(i.max_score for i in items
59	                        if i.max_score > 0 and i.id not in unverifiable)
60	    per_item = {}
61	    got = 0.0
62	    got_verif = 0.0
63	    for it in items:
64	        frac = max(0.0, min(1.0, float(awarded.get(it.id, 0.0))))
65	        pts = frac * it.max_score          # negative for penalty items
66	        unv = it.id in unverifiable
67	        per_item[it.id] = {"fraction": frac, "points": pts, "max": it.max_score,
68	                           "penalty": it.max_score < 0, "unverifiable": unv}
69	        got += pts
70	        if not unv:
71	            got_verif += pts
72	    norm = got / ceiling if ceiling else 0.0
73	    norm_verif = got_verif / ceiling_verif if ceiling_verif else 0.0
74	    return {
75	        "normalized": max(0.0, min(1.0, norm)),
76	        "normalized_verifiable_only": max(0.0, min(1.0, norm_verif)),
77	        "raw_normalized": norm,            # keep the unclamped value for diagnostics
78	        "points": got,
79	        "ceiling": ceiling,
80	        "n_unverifiable": len(unverifiable),
81	        "per_item": per_item,

"""Rubric loading and scorecard aggregation.

A rubric is a list of items, each worth `score` points for a binary/quality
criterion. The judge awards each item a fraction in [0,1] of its points; the
normalized rubric score is (sum of awarded points) / (sum of max points).
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path

from . import config


@dataclass
class RubricItem:
    id: str
    criterion: str
    max_score: float


def load_rubric(task_folder: str) -> list[RubricItem]:
    raw = json.loads((config.DATA / task_folder / "rubric.json").read_text())
    items = []
    for r in raw:
        items.append(RubricItem(
            id=r["rubric_item_id"],
            criterion=r["criterion"].strip(),
            max_score=float(r["score"]),
        ))
    return items


def positive_max(items: list[RubricItem]) -> float:
    """Best achievable score: all positive items earned, no penalties triggered."""
    return sum(i.max_score for i in items if i.max_score > 0)


def normalize(items: list[RubricItem], awarded: dict[str, float],
              unverifiable: set[str] | None = None) -> dict:
    """awarded: item_id -> fraction in [0,1] of the *extent the criterion is true*.

    For positive items, fraction is credit earned. For penalty items (negative
    points), fraction is how strongly the (bad) condition holds, so a triggered
    penalty subtracts points. The deliverable's score is divided by the positive
    ceiling and clamped to [0,1]; a deliverable that trips heavy penalties floors
    at 0 rather than going negative.

    `unverifiable` items (the judge couldn't check them at all) still count as 0
    in `normalized`, but are removed from BOTH numerator and denominator in
    `normalized_verifiable_only` — the fair score given what was actually checkable.
    A large gap between the two means the rubric asks for things no judge can
    verify from the deliverable (a coverage/verifiability defect).
    """
    unverifiable = unverifiable or set()
    ceiling = positive_max(items)
    ceiling_verif = sum(i.max_score for i in items
                        if i.max_score > 0 and i.id not in unverifiable)
    per_item = {}
    got = 0.0
    got_verif = 0.0
    for it in items:
        frac = max(0.0, min(1.0, float(awarded.get(it.id, 0.0))))
        pts = frac * it.max_score          # negative for penalty items
        unv = it.id in unverifiable
        per_item[it.id] = {"fraction": frac, "points": pts, "max": it.max_score,
                           "penalty": it.max_score < 0, "unverifiable": unv}
        got += pts
        if not unv:
            got_verif += pts
    norm = got / ceiling if ceiling else 0.0
    norm_verif = got_verif / ceiling_verif if ceiling_verif else 0.0
    return {
        "normalized": max(0.0, min(1.0, norm)),
        "normalized_verifiable_only": max(0.0, min(1.0, norm_verif)),
        "raw_normalized": norm,            # keep the unclamped value for diagnostics
        "points": got,
        "ceiling": ceiling,
        "n_unverifiable": len(unverifiable),
        "per_item": per_item,
    }

lastPrompt: help me brainstorm how to measure hackability of reward rubrics (axis 2)

content.text:
Good — I've got the shape of it now. You already have a "leaks" metric (null inputs scoring points) which is really an embryonic hackability measure. Let me build the brainstorm around that and the RL framing.

## The core reframe

"Hackable = score goes up without the work getting better." That sentence has two terms, and the whole axis lives or dies on the **second** one. You can always measure rubric score. The hard part is the independent quality signal you compare it against — and it **must not be the rubric itself**, or the measurement is circular.

So every hackability method below reduces to: *produce a deliverable that scores high on the rubric but low on an independent quality oracle.* Your free oracle is GDPval's gold + a holistic "is this actually good work?" judge that **never sees the rubric**. Hold onto that — it's the spine of the axis.

This also tells you the right experimental stance: **white-box**. In RL the gradient effectively sees the reward, so the worst-case-relevant test is a policy that sees the rubric verbatim and optimizes against it. Black-box (policy blind to rubric) measures *natural* hackability; white-box measures the *exploitable ceiling*. Default to white-box for the headline number.

## A taxonomy of hack patterns (the "more patterns" they left for you)

Presence-without-absence is pattern #1. Your `57b2cdf2` rubric is a goldmine of the others — it's ~45 items, almost all pure form:

1. **Unbounded presence** — "caption is visible" → make it giant. Presence verb, no bound/absence clause.
2. **Literal-string / form items** — "Contains a section labeled 'Summary'", "Lists case identifier 'SERC-1410PI-2025'". A template hits these with zero real work. (This is *most* of `57b2cdf2`.)
3. **Restating the brief** — "States the surveillance window was 9pm–1am." That fact is *in the prompt*. Echoing the input ≠ doing the work — this is exactly why your `unedited_input` null leaks 0.64.
4. **Self-certification** — "Confirms that the photographs correspond to the events." The policy writes "the photographs correspond to the events" and the judge believes the assertion. The deliverable grades its own homework.
5. **No-penalty / free-additions** — nothing punishes harmful content (covering the subject, fabricated events, hallucinated evidence). Anything not penalized is free to add. Count of penalty items here: **zero**.
6. **Monotonic / no-saturation** — reward scales with quantity (length, # transitions, # effects) with no excess penalty → policy spams.
7. **Point-mass on form** — independent additive items where the "real work" is 1–2 points out of 60. You hack 95% by nailing form and skipping the one item that's the actual task. (Look at `57b2cdf2`: ~40 form points vs a handful of substance points.)
8. **Proxy criteria** — grades a correlate ("uses timestamps") instead of the thing ("chronology is correct").

This taxonomy is your deliverable's intellectual core — and it's what the iterator targets.

## Three ways to turn it into a number

**A. Static classifier (cheap, per-item, drives the iterator).**
LLM labels each item with the pattern(s) above + a 0/1/2 hackability severity. Aggregate **weighted by point value** (points = the reward), so:

> `HI_static = (hackable points) / (total positive points)`

Cheap signals to ground it: n-gram overlap between the criterion's target fact and the prompt/reference files (catches #3), presence-verb-without-absence-clause regex (#1), "states/affirms/confirms that…" pattern (#4), penalty-item count (#5). Per-item flags feed the rewrite loop directly.

**B. Attack suite (empirical, reuses your null infra).**
Generalize "leaks" from do-nothing to a family of cheap attacks, score each, record which items each passes:
- *Null* (have it) — blank / unedited input.
- *Template-copy* — mechanically fill the form, copy every fact from the prompt, do no footage analysis.
- *Maxed-presence* — overdo every presence cue (giant captions, every transition).

Items passed by an attack but failed by an honest low-effort output = empirically hackable. This is concrete, reproducible, and basically already half-built.

**C. Adversarial optimizer (the gold-standard headline).**
One white-box "hacker" policy: rubric in context, instructed to max score with minimal real work. Then **best-of-N** (take max rubric score over N attempts) — best-of-N is a cheap stand-in for what RL would actually find. Score the hacker on *both* rubric and the rubric-blind quality oracle:

> `HI_emp = rubric_score(hacker) − quality_score(hacker)`

Big positive gap = lots of reward purchasable without quality = hackable. Attribute the hacker's gained points to specific items → those items get flagged → **same per-item attribution closes the iterator loop**: rewrite those items, re-run the hacker, confirm the gap shrank.

## Proposed scorecard design

Two columns, because they answer different questions:
- **HI_static** — *where* and *why* (per-item, point-weighted). Cheap, runs on all 10 incl. the 4 gold-free tasks.
- **HI_emp** — *is it actually exploitable* (defensible headline, needs the oracle). Run on the 6 gold tasks.
- Per-item: `hack_pattern`, `severity`, `leaked_by` (which attack), `points_at_risk`.

They should correlate; where they don't is interesting (static says hackable, optimizer couldn't exploit it → maybe the judge is robust; or vice versa → your classifier missed a pattern).

## What I'd actually build given the budget

You've got ~4 hrs of axis work and the null/leak infra already exists. I'd do **B then A then C**:

1. **Extend leaks → attack suite (B)** — you're 70% there; adds template-copy + maxed-presence attacks. ~Fast, reproducible, runs everywhere.
2. **Static classifier (A)** — one LLM pass, gives the taxonomy table + per-item flags the iterator consumes. Highest ratio of insight-to-cost.
3. **One white-box hacker + best-of-N (C)** on 2–3 tasks (lead with `57b2cdf2`) for the headline gap and the before/after iterator story.

The clean validation moment for the report: iterator adds an absence clause to a presence item → re-run the maxed-presence attack → that item now *fails* the hack while gold still passes. That's hackability provably dropping, which is exactly the before/after Part 3 asks for.

Want me to sketch the data model / module layout for the attack suite and the static classifier so it slots into your existing `eval/` structure?
yes sounds good let's go with option B given our time constraints. go ahead and assemble an attack suite per task that will be used in the downstream rubric iterator
Let me read the existing eval modules so the attack suite matches conventions and plugs into the pipeline.

1	"""Generate sensible 'do-nothing' deliverables for Axis 1 (boundary calibration).
2	
3	For each task with a gold deliverable we synthesize null variants that a lazy or
4	degenerate policy might emit. A well-calibrated rubric should score these near 0:
5	
6	  blank           a structurally-valid but empty artifact of the right TYPE
7	                  (blank PDF / black silent video / zip of silence)
8	  unedited_input  the raw input handed back with no work done
9	                  (the unrevised source doc as a PDF; the source audio re-zipped;
10	                   a black video carrying only the source audio track)
11	
12	These are task-aware but generated from generic rules keyed on the gold file
13	type, so they stay sensible without hand-coding every task.
14	"""
15	from __future__ import annotations
16	
17	import json
18	import shutil
19	import subprocess
20	import zipfile
21	from pathlib import Path
22	
23	import fitz
24	
25	from . import config
26	from .deliverable import extract_text, kind
27	
28	
29	def _run(cmd):
30	    subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL, stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)
31	
32	
33	def _blank_pdf(out: Path):
34	    doc = fitz.open()
35	    doc.new_page()  # one empty page
36	    doc.save(out)
37	
38	
39	def _text_pdf(text: str, out: Path):
40	    doc = fitz.open()
41	    page = doc.new_page()
42	    rect = fitz.Rect(50, 50, page.rect.width - 50, page.rect.height - 50)
43	    page.insert_textbox(rect, text[:4000], fontsize=9)
44	    doc.save(out)
45	
46	
47	def _black_video(out: Path, dur=5, audio: Path | None = None):
48	    cmd = ["ffmpeg", "-y", "-f", "lavfi", "-i", f"color=c=black:s=640x360:d={dur}"]
49	    if audio is not None:
50	        cmd += ["-i", str(audio), "-shortest"]
51	    else:
52	        cmd += ["-f", "lavfi", "-i", f"anullsrc=r=16000:cl=mono", "-t", str(dur)]
53	    cmd += ["-pix_fmt", "yuv420p", str(out)]
54	    _run(cmd)
55	
56	
57	def _silent_wav(out: Path, dur=5):
58	    _run(["ffmpeg", "-y", "-f", "lavfi", "-i", "anullsrc=r=16000:cl=mono",
59	          "-t", str(dur), str(out)])
60	
61	
62	def _zip_of(files: list[Path], out: Path):
63	    with zipfile.ZipFile(out, "w") as z:
64	        for f in files:
65	            z.write(f, arcname=f.name)
66	
67	
68	def _task(task_folder: str) -> dict:
69	    return json.loads((config.DATA / task_folder / "task.json").read_text())
70	
71	
72	def build_nulls(task_folder: str) -> dict[str, Path]:
73	    """Create null bundles on disk; return {variant_name: directory}."""
74	    meta = _task(task_folder)
75	    dels = meta.get("deliverable_files") or []
76	    if not dels:
77	        return {}  # no gold -> Axis 1 not applicable
78	
79	    gold_type = kind(dels[0]["name"])
80	    refs = [config.DATA / task_folder / "reference_files" / Path(r["name"]).name
81	            for r in meta.get("reference_files", [])]
82	    refs = [r for r in refs if r.exists()]
83	
84	    base = config.NULLS / task_folder
85	    variants: dict[str, Path] = {}
86	
87	    def mkdir(name: str) -> Path:
88	        d = base / name
89	        if d.exists():
90	            shutil.rmtree(d)
91	        d.mkdir(parents=True)
92	        return d
93	
94	    if gold_type == "document":  # gold is a PDF
95	        d = mkdir("blank"); _blank_pdf(d / "blank.pdf"); variants["blank"] = d
96	        src = next((r for r in refs if r.suffix.lower() in (".docx", ".pdf", ".txt")), None)
97	        if src:
98	            d = mkdir("unedited_input")
99	            _text_pdf(extract_text(src), d / "unedited_input.pdf")
100	            variants["unedited_input"] = d
101	
102	    elif gold_type == "video":  # gold is an mp4
103	        d = mkdir("blank"); _black_video(d / "black.mp4"); variants["blank"] = d
104	        aud = next((r for r in refs if r.suffix.lower() in (".mp3", ".wav", ".m4a")), None)
105	        vid = next((r for r in refs if r.suffix.lower() in (".mp4", ".mov")), None)
106	        if aud:
107	            d = mkdir("unedited_input")
108	            _black_video(d / "raw_audio_over_black.mp4", dur=10, audio=aud)
109	            variants["unedited_input"] = d
110	        elif vid:
111	            d = mkdir("unedited_input")
112	            shutil.copy(vid, d / Path(vid).name)
113	            variants["unedited_input"] = d
114	
115	    elif gold_type == "archive":  # gold is a zip (e.g. audio stems)
116	        d = mkdir("blank")
117	        _silent_wav(d / "silence.wav")
118	        _zip_of([d / "silence.wav"], d / "blank_stems.zip")
119	        (d / "silence.wav").unlink()
120	        variants["blank"] = d
121	        src_audio = [r for r in refs if r.suffix.lower() in (".wav", ".mp3")]
122	        if src_audio:
123	            d = mkdir("unedited_input")
124	            _zip_of(src_audio, d / "raw_input_stems.zip")
125	            variants["unedited_input"] = d
126	
127	    return variants
128	
129	
130	if __name__ == "__main__":
131	    import sys
132	    for tf in sys.argv[1:] or [p.name for p in config.DATA.iterdir() if p.is_dir()]:
133	        v = build_nulls(tf)
134	        print(f"{tf}: {list(v) or 'no gold -> skipped'}")

file.content:
"""Generate sensible 'do-nothing' deliverables for Axis 1 (boundary calibration).

For each task with a gold deliverable we synthesize null variants that a lazy or
degenerate policy might emit. A well-calibrated rubric should score these near 0:

  blank           a structurally-valid but empty artifact of the right TYPE
                  (blank PDF / black silent video / zip of silence)
  unedited_input  the raw input handed back with no work done
                  (the unrevised source doc as a PDF; the source audio re-zipped;
                   a black video carrying only the source audio track)

These are task-aware but generated from generic rules keyed on the gold file
type, so they stay sensible without hand-coding every task.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import shutil
import subprocess
import zipfile
from pathlib import Path

import fitz

from . import config
from .deliverable import extract_text, kind


def _run(cmd):
    subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL, stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)


def _blank_pdf(out: Path):
    doc = fitz.open()
    doc.new_page()  # one empty page
    doc.save(out)


def _text_pdf(text: str, out: Path):
    doc = fitz.open()
    page = doc.new_page()
    rect = fitz.Rect(50, 50, page.rect.width - 50, page.rect.height - 50)
    page.insert_textbox(rect, text[:4000], fontsize=9)
    doc.save(out)


def _black_video(out: Path, dur=5, audio: Path | None = None):
    cmd = ["ffmpeg", "-y", "-f", "lavfi", "-i", f"color=c=black:s=640x360:d={dur}"]
    if audio is not None:
        cmd += ["-i", str(audio), "-shortest"]
    else:
        cmd += ["-f", "lavfi", "-i", f"anullsrc=r=16000:cl=mono", "-t", str(dur)]
    cmd += ["-pix_fmt", "yuv420p", str(out)]
    _run(cmd)


def _silent_wav(out: Path, dur=5):
    _run(["ffmpeg", "-y", "-f", "lavfi", "-i", "anullsrc=r=16000:cl=mono",
          "-t", str(dur), str(out)])


def _zip_of(files: list[Path], out: Path):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(out, "w") as z:
        for f in files:
            z.write(f, arcname=f.name)


def _task(task_folder: str) -> dict:
    return json.loads((config.DATA / task_folder / "task.json").read_text())


def build_nulls(task_folder: str) -> dict[str, Path]:
    """Create null bundles on disk; return {variant_name: directory}."""
    meta = _task(task_folder)
    dels = meta.get("deliverable_files") or []
    if not dels:
        return {}  # no gold -> Axis 1 not applicable

    gold_type = kind(dels[0]["name"])
    refs = [config.DATA / task_folder / "reference_files" / Path(r["name"]).name
            for r in meta.get("reference_files", [])]
    refs = [r for r in refs if r.exists()]

    base = config.NULLS / task_folder
    variants: dict[str, Path] = {}

    def mkdir(name: str) -> Path:
        d = base / name
        if d.exists():
            shutil.rmtree(d)
        d.mkdir(parents=True)
        return d

    if gold_type == "document":  # gold is a PDF
        d = mkdir("blank"); _blank_pdf(d / "blank.pdf"); variants["blank"] = d
        src = next((r for r in refs if r.suffix.lower() in (".docx", ".pdf", ".txt")), None)
        if src:
            d = mkdir("unedited_input")
            _text_pdf(extract_text(src), d / "unedited_input.pdf")
            variants["unedited_input"] = d

    elif gold_type == "video":  # gold is an mp4
        d = mkdir("blank"); _black_video(d / "black.mp4"); variants["blank"] = d
        aud = next((r for r in refs if r.suffix.lower() in (".mp3", ".wav", ".m4a")), None)
        vid = next((r for r in refs if r.suffix.lower() in (".mp4", ".mov")), None)
        if aud:
            d = mkdir("unedited_input")
            _black_video(d / "raw_audio_over_black.mp4", dur=10, audio=aud)
            variants["unedited_input"] = d
        elif vid:
            d = mkdir("unedited_input")
            shutil.copy(vid, d / Path(vid).name)
            variants["unedited_input"] = d

    elif gold_type == "archive":  # gold is a zip (e.g. audio stems)
        d = mkdir("blank")
        _silent_wav(d / "silence.wav")
        _zip_of([d / "silence.wav"], d / "blank_stems.zip")
        (d / "silence.wav").unlink()
        variants["blank"] = d
        src_audio = [r for r in refs if r.suffix.lower() in (".wav", ".mp3")]
        if src_audio:
            d = mkdir("unedited_input")
            _zip_of(src_audio, d / "raw_input_stems.zip")
            variants["unedited_input"] = d

    return variants


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    for tf in sys.argv[1:] or [p.name for p in config.DATA.iterdir() if p.is_dir()]:
        v = build_nulls(tf)
        print(f"{tf}: {list(v) or 'no gold -> skipped'}")

file.content:
"""Shared paths, env loading, and cost tracking for the rubric-grader eval suite."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import threading
from pathlib import Path

ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
DATA = ROOT / "data"
RESULTS = ROOT / "eval" / "results"
NULLS = ROOT / "eval" / "null_inputs"
CACHE = ROOT / "eval" / ".cache"  # transcripts, extracted frames, etc.

for _d in (RESULTS, NULLS, CACHE):
    _d.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)


def _load_env() -> None:
    """Minimal .env loader (avoids a python-dotenv dependency)."""
    env = ROOT / ".env"
    if not env.exists():
        return
    for line in env.read_text().splitlines():
        line = line.strip()
        if not line or line.startswith("#") or "=" not in line:
            continue
        k, v = line.split("=", 1)
        os.environ.setdefault(k.strip(), v.strip())


_load_env()

OPENAI_API_KEY = os.environ.get("OPENAI_API_KEY", "")
OPENROUTER_API_KEY = os.environ.get("OPENROUTER_API_KEY", "")


class CostTracker:
    """Cheap, approximate spend tracker so a run never silently burns credits."""

    _lock = threading.Lock()
    prompt_tokens = 0
    completion_tokens = 0
    usd = 0.0
    calls = 0

    @classmethod
    def add(cls, prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int, in_price: float, out_price: float) -> None:
        with cls._lock:
            cls.prompt_tokens += prompt_tokens
            cls.completion_tokens += completion_tokens
            cls.usd += prompt_tokens * in_price + completion_tokens * out_price
            cls.calls += 1

    @classmethod
    def summary(cls) -> str:
        return (
            f"[cost] calls={cls.calls} "
            f"prompt={cls.prompt_tokens:,} completion={cls.completion_tokens:,} "
            f"~${cls.usd:.4f}"
        )

1	"""Unified eval runner — one entrypoint for the rubric scorecard.
2	
3	Two modes, selected with --mode:
4	
5	  rollout   Have policy model(s) attempt the real GDPval tasks and produce actual
6	            deliverables (eval.policy). These become gradeable subjects.
7	
8	  judge     Grade one or more SUBJECTS per task against the rubric, then derive the
9	            axes from a single judging matrix:
10	              * Boundary calibration (Axis 1): gold should score ~1, nulls ~0; we
11	                report the gold-null gap and "leaks" (null passing a positive item).
12	              * Cross-model consistency (Axis 3): when >=2 --judge-models grade the
13	                same subject, do they agree?
14	              * Self-consistency (Axis 3): when --repeats > 1, one judge re-grades the
15	                same subject; do the scores reproduce?
16	
17	A subject is named on the CLI: `gold`, a null variant (`blank`, `unedited_input`),
18	`rollout:<policy_key>`, or `preexisting:<path>`. Default subjects are gold + nulls
19	(the boundary anchors).
20	
21	Examples:
22	  # boundary + cross-model in one shot (3 judges, all gold tasks):
23	  python -m eval.run --mode judge --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
24	
25	  # add self-consistency (one judge, 5 repeats) on a single task:
26	  python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2 \
27	      --judge-models qwen-vl --repeats 5 --temperature 0.7
28	
29	  # consistency OFF (single judge, single pass) on a rollout you generated:
30	  python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:gpt-mini --judge-models gpt-mini
31	
32	  # generate rollouts first:
33	  python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini --tasks <folder>
34	"""
35	from __future__ import annotations
36	
37	import argparse
38	import itertools
39	import json
40	
41	from . import config, policy, run_common
42	from .config import CostTracker
43	from .harness import judge_safe as judge  # never let one flaky judgement abort the sweep
44	from .models import resolve
45	from .rubric import RubricItem, load_rubric
46	from .run_common import NULL_HACK_SCORE, agreement as _agreement, leaks as _leaks
47	
48	
49	# --- judge mode -------------------------------------------------------------
50	def run_judge(tasks, subjects_arg, judge_keys, repeats, self_key, temperature, verbose):
51	    judges = resolve(judge_keys)
52	    self_model = resolve([self_key])[0] if self_key else judges[0]
53	    report: dict = {}
54	    score_rows, cons_rows = [], []
55	
56	    for tf in tasks:
57	        subjects = subjects_arg or run_common.available_subjects(tf)
58	        if not subjects:
59	            print(f"skip {tf}: no subjects (no gold/nulls; pass --subjects)"); continue
60	        print(f"\n=== {tf} ===  subjects={subjects}")
61	        task = run_common.load_task(tf)
62	        items = load_rubric(tf)
63	        report[tf] = {"subjects": {}}
64	
65	        # matrix[subject][judge_key] = base run (T=0); matrix[subject]['_self'] = repeats
66	        matrix: dict[str, dict] = {}
67	        for subj in subjects:
68	            try:
69	                bundle = run_common.resolve_subject(tf, subj)
70	            except SystemExit as e:
71	                print(f"  skip subject {subj}: {e}"); continue
72	            print(f"  -- subject '{subj}' ({len(bundle.files)} files)")
73	            base = {}
74	            for m in judges:
75	                r = judge(m, bundle, items, task["prompt"], temperature=0.0, verbose=verbose)
76	                base[m.key] = r
77	                vo = r.get("normalized_verifiable_only", r["normalized"])
78	                flags = ""
79	                if not r.get("valid", True):
80	                    flags += f" INVALID(scored {r.get('n_items_scored', 0)}/{len(items)})"
81	                if r.get("penalty_floored"):
82	                    flags += " PENALTY-FLOORED"
83	                if r.get("error"):
84	                    flags += f" ERROR({r['error'][:40]})"
85	                print(f"     [{m.key}] score={r['normalized']:.2f} "
86	                      f"(verifiable-only={vo:.2f}, unverifiable={r.get('n_unverifiable', 0)}){flags}")
87	                score_rows.append([tf, subj, m.key, round(r["normalized"], 3),
88	                                   round(vo, 3), r.get("n_unverifiable", 0), len(items),
89	                                   r.get("valid", True), r.get("penalty_floored", False)])
90	            self_runs = []
91	            if repeats > 1:
92	                self_runs = [judge(self_model, bundle, items, task["prompt"],
93	                                   temperature=temperature, verbose=verbose)
94	                             for _ in range(repeats)]
95	            matrix[subj] = {"base": base, "self": self_runs}
96	            report[tf]["subjects"][subj] = {
97	                "is_null": run_common.is_null_subject(tf, subj),
98	                "base": base,
99	            }
100	
101	        # ---- derive: self-consistency (per subject) ----
102	        if repeats > 1:
103	            for subj, cell in matrix.items():
104	                if not cell["self"]:
105	                    continue
106	                ag = _agreement(items, cell["self"])
107	                report[tf]["subjects"][subj]["self_consistency"] = {
108	                    "model": self_model.key, "repeats": repeats, "temperature": temperature, **ag}
109	                print(f"  self-consistency [{self_model.key} x{repeats}] on '{subj}': "
110	                      f"scores={ag['total_scores']} stdev={ag['total_stdev']} "
111	                      f"unanimous={ag['frac_items_unanimous']:.0%}")
112	                cons_rows.append([tf, subj, "self", f"{self_model.key}x{repeats}",
113	                                  ag["total_mean"], ag["total_stdev"],
114	                                  ag["frac_items_unanimous"], ag["mean_item_stdev"]])
115	
116	        # ---- derive: cross-model consistency (per subject) ----
117	        if len(judges) > 1:
118	            for subj, cell in matrix.items():
119	                runs = list(cell["base"].values())
120	                ag = _agreement(items, runs)
121	                scores = {k: round(r["normalized"], 3) for k, r in cell["base"].items()}
122	                pair = {f"{a}-{b}": round(abs(scores[a] - scores[b]), 3)
123	                        for a, b in itertools.combinations(scores, 2)}
124	                report[tf]["subjects"][subj]["cross_model"] = {
125	                    "models": list(scores), "scores": scores, "pairwise_gap": pair, **ag}
126	                print(f"  cross-model on '{subj}': scores={scores} "
127	                      f"stdev={ag['total_stdev']} unanimous={ag['frac_items_unanimous']:.0%}")
128	                cons_rows.append([tf, subj, "cross", "+".join(scores),
129	                                  ag["total_mean"], ag["total_stdev"],
130	                                  ag["frac_items_unanimous"], ag["mean_item_stdev"]])
131	
132	        # ---- derive: boundary calibration (per judge, needs gold + a null) ----
133	        nulls = [s for s in matrix if report[tf]["subjects"][s]["is_null"]]
134	        if "gold" in matrix and nulls:
135	            report[tf]["boundary"] = {}
136	            for m in judges:
137	                gold_r = matrix["gold"]["base"][m.key]
138	                null_runs = [matrix[s]["base"][m.key] for s in nulls]
139	                worst_null = max((r["normalized"] for r in null_runs), default=0.0)
140	                gap = gold_r["normalized"] - worst_null
141	                leaks = _leaks(items, null_runs)
142	                report[tf]["boundary"][m.key] = {
143	                    "gold": round(gold_r["normalized"], 3),
144	                    "worst_null": round(worst_null, 3), "gap": round(gap, 3),
145	                    "leaks": leaks}
146	                print(f"  boundary [{m.key}]: gold={gold_r['normalized']:.2f} "
147	                      f"worst_null={worst_null:.2f} gap={gap:+.2f} leaks={len(leaks)}")
148	
149	    _write_outputs(report, score_rows, cons_rows)
150	    print(CostTracker.summary())
151	
152	
153	def _hack_flag(report, tf, subj, judge_key, norm) -> str:
154	    """Mark reward-hacking suspects: leaks on gold, or a null scoring too high."""
155	    is_null = report[tf]["subjects"][subj]["is_null"]
156	    if is_null and norm > NULL_HACK_SCORE:
157	        return "null-scores-high"
158	    if subj == "gold":
159	        n = len(report.get(tf, {}).get("boundary", {}).get(judge_key, {}).get("leaks", []))
160	        if n:
161	            return f"{n}-leaks"
162	    return ""
163	
164	
165	def _write_outputs(report, score_rows, cons_rows):
166	    out_json = config.RESULTS / "scorecard.json"
167	    out_json.write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
168	    print(f"\nwrote {out_json.relative_to(config.ROOT)}")
169	
170	    # annotate score rows with boundary gap + reward-hack flag
171	    full_rows = []
172	    for r in score_rows:
173	        tf, subj, jk, norm = r[0], r[1], r[2], r[3]
174	        gap = ""
175	        if subj == "gold":
176	            b = report.get(tf, {}).get("boundary", {}).get(jk)
177	            if b:
178	                gap = b["gap"]
179	        full_rows.append(r + [gap, _hack_flag(report, tf, subj, jk, norm)])
180	
181	    run_common.write_tsv(
182	        config.RESULTS / "scorecard.tsv",
183	        ["task", "subject", "judge", "score", "verifiable_only", "n_unverifiable",
184	         "n_items", "valid", "penalty_floored", "gold_gap", "reward_hack_flag"],
185	        full_rows)
186	    if cons_rows:
187	        run_common.write_tsv(
188	            config.RESULTS / "consistency.tsv",
189	            ["task", "subject", "kind", "judges", "mean_score", "score_stdev",
190	             "frac_unanimous", "mean_item_stdev"],
191	            cons_rows)
192	
193	
194	# --- rollout mode -----------------------------------------------------------
195	def run_rollout(tasks, policy_keys, verbose, force=False):
196	    policies = resolve(policy_keys)
197	    rows = []
198	    for tf in tasks:
199	        print(f"\n=== {tf} ===")
200	        for m in policies:
201	            try:
202	                res = policy.rollout(m, tf, verbose=verbose, force=force)
203	            except Exception as e:  # one model/task failing must not abort the batch
204	                print(f"  [{m.key}] {tf}: ERROR -> {str(e)[:160]}")
205	                rows.append([tf, m.key, False, 0, f"(error: {str(e)[:80]})"])
206	                continue
207	            rows.append([tf, m.key, res["submitted"], res["rounds"],
208	                         "|".join(res["deliverable_files"]) or "(none)"])
209	    run_common.write_tsv(
210	        config.RESULTS / "rollouts.tsv",
211	        ["task", "policy", "submitted", "rounds", "deliverable_files"],
212	        rows)
213	    print(CostTracker.summary())
214	    print("\nGrade them with, e.g.:")
215	    print(f"  python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:{policies[0].key} "
216	          f"--judge-models {' '.join(policy_keys)}")
217	
218	
219	# --- CLI --------------------------------------------------------------------
220	def main():
221	    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Unified rubric-scorecard eval runner.")
222	    ap.add_argument("--mode", choices=["judge", "rollout", "iterate"], default="judge")
223	    ap.add_argument("--tasks", nargs="*", default=None,
224	                    help="task folders; default = all (rollout) / all with gold (judge, iterate)")
225	    ap.add_argument("--verbose", action="store_true")
226	
227	    # judge mode
228	    ap.add_argument("--subjects", nargs="*", default=None,
229	                    help="what to grade: gold | blank | unedited_input | rollout:<key> | "
230	                         "preexisting:<path>. Default = gold + available nulls.")
231	    ap.add_argument("--judge-models", nargs="*", default=["gpt-mini"],
232	                    help=">=2 models turns ON cross-model consistency.")
233	    ap.add_argument("--repeats", type=int, default=1,
234	                    help=">1 turns ON self-consistency (one judge, repeated).")
235	    ap.add_argument("--self-model", default=None,
236	                    help="judge used for self-consistency repeats (default: first --judge-model).")
237	    ap.add_argument("--temperature", type=float, default=0.7,
238	                    help="temperature for self-consistency repeats.")
239	
240	    # rollout mode
241	    ap.add_argument("--policy-models", nargs="*", default=["gpt-mini"],
242	                    help="model(s) that attempt the tasks to generate deliverables.")
243	    ap.add_argument("--force", action="store_true",
244	                    help="rollout mode: regenerate even if a deliverable already exists.")
245	
246	    # iterate mode (the rubric-improvement loop; reuses --judge-models / --repeats /
247	    # --self-model / --temperature / --subjects as the per-version evaluation config)
248	    ap.add_argument("--rounds", type=int, default=1,
249	                    help="iterate mode: max rewrite rounds per task (default 1).")
250	    ap.add_argument("--rewrite-model", default="gpt-mini",
251	                    help="iterate mode: model that rewrites flagged criteria.")
252	    a = ap.parse_args()
253	
254	    if a.mode == "rollout":
255	        tasks = a.tasks or run_common.all_tasks()
256	        run_rollout(tasks, a.policy_models, a.verbose, a.force)
257	    elif a.mode == "iterate":
258	        from . import iterate
259	        tasks = a.tasks or run_common.tasks_with_gold()
260	        iterate.run_iterate(tasks, a.rounds, a.judge_models, a.self_model,
261	                            a.repeats, a.temperature, a.rewrite_model,
262	                            a.subjects, a.verbose)
263	    else:
264	        tasks = a.tasks or run_common.tasks_with_gold()
265	        run_judge(tasks, a.subjects, a.judge_models, a.repeats,
266	                  a.self_model, a.temperature, a.verbose)
267	
268	
269	if __name__ == "__main__":
270	    main()
271
